content.full_text:
REMCHO, JOHANSEN & PURCELL
ATTORNEYS AT LAW

220 MONTGOMERY STREET, SUITE 800
SAN FRANCISCO, CALIFORNIA 94104
415/398-6230    FAX: 415/398-7256

November 21, 1990


Fair Political Practices Commission
428 J Street, Suite 600
Sacramento, CA  95814

                    Re:  Assembly Member-Elect Barbara Lee

Dear Commission:

     I am writing on behalf of Assembly Member-Elect
Barbara Lee to request formal written advice pursuant to
Government Code section 83114(b) and Regulation 18329(b) on
the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform
Act.  Ms. Lee has authorized my law firm to represent her and
make this request on her behalf.  Ms. Lee's mailing address
is:

               Barbara Lee
               Lee Associates
               1350 Franklin Street,  Suite 250
               Oakland, CA  94612

     Barbara Lee was elected to the California State
Assembly on November 6, 1990 and will take office December 3,
1990.  Ms. Lee is the sole owner of Lee Associates, a
management consulting firm.  Ms. Lee requests advice
regarding the application of the Act to decisions involving
two of her firm's clients, the California State Department of
Transportation and the Committee for Congressman Ronald V.
Dellums.

QUESTION I:  Do the conflict of interest provisions of the
Political Reform Act, including the Act's administrative
remedies, apply to Ms. Lee's participating in or attending
to influence decisions involving the Department of
Transportation for the 12 months after she last receives
payment from the Department?

     Lee Associates' work for the state Department of
Transportation consisted of monitoring an affirmative action
program of a freeway construction project, conducting an
on-the-job training placement effectiveness evaluation, and
identifying potential funding sources for pre-apprenticeship

Fair Political Practices Commission
November 21, 1990
Page 2

training, basic skills enhancement and support services for
the highway construction industry. Ms. Lee has received over
$250 from the Department of Transportation through Lee
Associates in the last 12 months, and Ms. Lee's interest in
the firm is greater than ten percent. See Gov. Code § 
87103(c); § 82030. Lee Associates will have completed the
work on the contract for the Department of Transportation
before Ms. Lee takes office. Lee Associates will receive the
final payment on the contract after Ms. Lee takes office.
However, Lee Associates' contractual right to the final
payment will not be contingent upon any act, event or
determination to be made after Ms. Lee has taken office.

     The following specific questions arise:

     A.     We understand that the conflict of interest
provisions of the Political Reform Act apply only if the
governmental decision affects a "financial interest" of the
official. Gov. Code § 87100; In re Owen (June 2, 1976) 2
FPPC Ops. 77, 79-80. Does Ms. Lee have a "financial
interest" vis-a-vis the Department of Transportation?

          (1)    Is the money Ms. Lee has received
from the Department of Transportation via
Lee Associates excluded from the
definition of "income" by analogy to the
exclusion of salary from state agencies
under Government Code section
82030(b)(2)?

          (2)    Does Regulation 18704 regarding
income from former employers or the
reasoning behind it preclude
classification of the Department of
Transportation as a source of income to
Ms. Lee?

     B.     It is our understanding that the conflict of
interest provisions of the Act apply only if the effect of
the decision on the source of income is "distinguishable from
its effect on the public generally." Gov. Code § 87103; In
re Owen, supra, at 79-80. Further, we understand that under
the so-called "public generally" exception the "public
generally" for a state legislator includes all residents of
the state. See In Re Leden (August 20, 1985) 9 FPPC Ops. 1,
12 (public consists of "entire jurisdiction" of agency). The
FPPC has stated that if a public official's source of income
is a state agency, "the effect of the decision will flow to
all residents of the state." FPPC Informal Assistance Letter
I-90-128A, at 9 (July 9, 1990). Consequently, the effect of

Fair Political Practices Commission
November 21, 1990
Page 3

any legislative decision by a state legislator involving a
state agency would be indistinguishable from the effect on
the public generally, and disqualification would not be
required. See ibid at 9.

Under this reasoning, does the "public generally"
exception exempt legislative decisions by Ms. Lee affecting
the Department of Transportation from the conflict of
interest requirements of the Act?

C. It is our understanding that the Act only
applies to decisions that have a "material financial effect"
on the official's source of income. Gov. Code § 87103 In re
Owen, supra, at 79-80. If neither of the above stated
exclusions exempts decisions regarding the Department of
Transportation from the Act, which decisions involving the
Department will have a "material" effect on it?

(1) The FPPC has stated that the
regulations regarding nonprofit entities,
2 Cal. Code Regs. § 18702.5, apply to
decisions indirectly involving
governmental agencies. See FPPC Informal
Assistance Letter I-90-128A, supra at 8
(applying section 18702.5 to government
agencies). Consequently, would section
18702.5 apply to decisions that
indirectly involve the Department of
Transportation here?

(2) Would the following legislative
decisions have a "material financial
effect" on the Department of
Transportation:

a. Decisions regarding rerouting
the Cypress freeway in Oakland and other
transportation issues related to the
October 17, 1989 earthquake.

b. Decisions regarding
participation of minority and women-owned
and managed businesses in work contracted
by the Department of Transportation.

c. Decisions regarding affirmative
action within the Department of
Transportation.

Fair Political Practices Commission
November 21, 1990
Page 4

     d. Decisions regarding the
Department of Transportation's
apprenticeship program.

     e. Decisions involving public
transportation concerns expressed by Ms.
Lee's constituents, e.g. increased access
to public transportation and increased
use of shuttles.

     D. If the Political Reform Act requires Ms. Lee to
disqualify herself from some or all decisions involving the
Department of Transportation, do the remedies of Chapter 3 of
the Act apply?

     It is our understanding that at present none of the
administrative, civil, or criminal remedies of the Political
Reform Act applies to members of the Legislature. Effective
January 1, 1991, the administrative remedies of the Act will
apply to members of the Legislature under limited
circumstances.

     However, under the recently passed Senate Bill 1738
(the "Ethics Bill"), the remedies would only apply to
legislation that is "nongeneral legislation." The statute
defines "nongeneral legislation" as legislation: 1) that is
not of a general nature as defined by section 16 of article
IV of the California Constitution, Gov. Code
§ 87102.5(b)(4)); and 2) for which "[i]t is reasonably
foreseeable that [it] will have direct and significant
financial impact on one or more identifiable persons, or one
or more identifiable pieces of real property," but where it
is not "reasonably foreseeable that the legislation will have
a similar impact on the public generally or on a significant
segment of the public." Gov. Code § 87102.6(a). "More than
a small number of persons or pieces of real property"
constitutes a "significant segment of the public." Gov. Code
§ 87102.6(b)(5).

     (1) Does legislation that has a material
effect on the Department of
Transportation affect all residents of
the state and thus have a similar impact
on the public generally?

     (2) Does legislation that has a material
effect on the Department of
Transportation not have a similar impact
on "more than a small number of persons?"

Fair Political Practices Commission
November 21, 1990
Page 5

     (3)  Is legislation that has a material
effect on the Department of
Transportation of a general nature as
defined by the California Constitution or
otherwise not nongeneral legislation as
defined by the Ethics Bill?

QUESTION II: Do the conflict of interest provisions of the
Political Reform Act, including the Act's administrative
remedies, apply to Ms. Lee's participating in or attempting
to influence decisions regarding Congressional
reapportionment for 12 months after she last received payment
from the Committee for Congressman Ronald V. Dellums?

     The Committee for Congressman Ronald V. Dellums has
paid Lee Associates for fundraising work the firm has done
for it. The committee is Congressman Dellum's principal
campaign committee under federal law. Ms. Lee has received
over $250 from the committee through Lee Associates in the
last 12 months, and, as stated above, Ms. Lee's interest in
the firm is greater than ten percent. See Gov. Code §
87103(c); § 82030.

     The following specific questions arise:

     A.  We understand that the Act applies only if it
is "reasonably foreseeable" that the decision will have a
"material financial effect" on the source of income to the
official. Gov. Code § 87103; In re Owen, supra, at 79-80.
Is it "reasonably foreseeable" that Congressional
reapportionment will have a "material financial effect" on
the Committee for Congressman Ronald V. Dellums?

     B.  If so, will decisions regarding Congressional
reapportionment affect the Dellums Committee in a manner that
is indistinguishable from its effect on the public generally?

     C.  If the Political Reform Act requires Ms. Lee
to disqualify herself from any of these decisions, do the
remedies of Chapter 3 of the Act apply pursuant to Government
Code sections 87102.5 - 87102.6?

          (1)  Will the effect of Congressional
reapportionment on the Dellums Committee
be similar to its effect on the public
generally?

          (2)  Will Congressional reapportionment
have an impact on "more than a small

Fair Political Practices Commission
November 21, 1990
Page 6

number of persons" that is similar to its
impact on the Dellums Committee?

(3) Is Congressional reapportionment
legislation of a general nature under the
California Constitution or otherwise not
nongeneral legislation as defined by the
Ethics Bill?

We appreciate your prompt attention to these
matters.

Sincerely,

John Lewis

JAL/bk

I: Do the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act, including the Act's administrative remedies, apply to Ms. Lee's participating in or attempting to influence decisions involving the Department of Transportation for the 12 months after she last receives payment from the Department?

Lee Associates' work for the state Department of Transportation consisted of monitoring an affirmative action program of a freeway construction project, conducting an on-the-job training placement effectiveness evaluation, and identifying potential funding sources for pre-apprenticeship

Fair Political Practices Commission  
November 21, 1990  

training, basic skills enhancement and support services for the highway construction industry. Ms. Lee has received over $250 from the Department of Transportation through Lee Associates in the last 12 months, and Ms. Lee's interest in the firm is greater than ten percent. See Gov. Code § 87103(c); § 82030. Lee Associates will have completed the work on the contract for the Department of Transportation before Ms. Lee takes office. Lee Associates will receive the final payment on the contract after Ms. Lee takes office. However, Lee Associates' contractual right to the final payment will not be contingent upon any act, event, or determination to be made after Ms. Lee has taken office.

The following specific questions arise:

A. We understand that the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act apply only if the governmental decision affects a "financial interest" of the official. Gov. Code § 87100; In re Owen (June 2, 1976) 2 FPPC Ops. 77, 79-80. Does Ms. Lee have a "financial interest" vis-a-vis the Department of Transportation?

(1) Is the money Ms. Lee has received from the Department of Transportation via Lee Associates excluded from the definition of "income" by analogy to the exclusion of salary from state agencies under Government Code section 82030(b)(2)?

(2) Does Regulation 18704 regarding income from former employers or the reasoning behind it preclude classification of the Department of Transportation as a source of income to Ms. Lee?

B. It is our understanding that the conflict of interest provisions of the Act apply only if the effect of the decision on the source of income is "distinguishable from its effect on the public generally." Gov. Code § 87103; In re Owen, supra, at 79-80. Further, we understand that under the so-called "public generally" exception the "public generally" for a state legislator includes all residents of the state. See In Re Legan (August 20, 1985) 9 FPPC Ops. 1, 12 (public consists of "entire jurisdiction" of agency). The FPPC has stated that if a public official's source of income is a state agency, "the effect of the decision will flow to all residents of the state." FPPC Informal Assistance Letter I-90-128A, at 9 (July 9, 1990). Consequently, the effect of

Fair Political Practices Commission
November 21, 1990

any legislative decision by a state legislator involving a state agency would be indistinguishable from the effect on the public generally, and disqualification would not be required. See ibid at 9.

Under this reasoning, does the "public generally" exception exempt legislative decisions by Ms. Lee affecting the Department of Transportation from the conflict of interest requirements of the Act?

C. It is our understanding that the Act only applies to decisions that have a "material financial effect" on the official's source of income. Gov. Code § 87103; In re Owen, supra, at 79-80. If neither of the above stated exclusions exempts decisions regarding the Department of Transportation from the Act, which decisions involving the Department will have a "material" effect on it?

(1) The FPPC has stated that the regulations regarding nonprofit entities, 2 Cal. Code Regs. § 18702.5, apply to decisions indirectly involving governmental agencies. See FPPC Informal Assistance Letter I-90-128A, supra at 8 (applying section 18702.5 to government agencies). Consequently, would section 18702.5 apply to decisions that indirectly involve the Department of Transportation here?

(2) Would the following legislative decisions have a "material financial effect" on the Department of Transportation:

a. Decisions regarding rerouting the Cypress freeway in Oakland and other transportation issues related to the October 17, 1989 earthquake.

b. Decisions regarding participation of minority and women-owned and managed businesses in work contracted by the Department of Transportation.

c. Decisions regarding affirmative action within the Department of Transportation.

Fair Political Practices Commission  
November 21, 1990  

d. Decisions regarding the Department of Transportation's apprenticeship program.

e. Decisions involving public transportation concerns expressed by Ms. Lee's constituents, e.g. increased access to public transportation and increased use of shuttles.

D. If the Political Reform Act requires Ms. Lee to disqualify herself from some or all decisions involving the Department of Transportation, do the remedies of Chapter 3 of the Act apply?

It is our understanding that at present none of the administrative, civil, or criminal remedies of the Political Reform Act applies to members of the Legislature. Effective January 1, 1991, the administrative remedies of the Act will apply to members of the Legislature under limited circumstances.

However, under the recently passed Senate Bill 1738 (the "Ethics Bill"), the remedies would only apply to legislation that is "nongeneral legislation." The statute defines "nongeneral legislation" as legislation: 1) that is not of a general nature as defined by section 16 of article IV of the California Constitution, Gov. Code § 87102.5(b)(4)); and 2) for which "[i]t is reasonably foreseeable that [it] will have direct and significant financial impact on one or more identifiable persons, or one or more identifiable pieces of real property," but where it is not "reasonably foreseeable that the legislation will have a similar impact on the public generally or on a significant segment of the public." Gov. Code § 87102.6(a). "More than a small number of persons or pieces of real property" constitutes a "significant segment of the public." Gov. Code § 87102.6(b)(5).

(1) Does legislation that has a material effect on the Department of Transportation affect all residents of the state and thus have a similar impact on the public generally?

(2) Does legislation that has a material effect on the Department of Transportation not have a similar impact on "more than a small number of persons?"

Fair Political Practices Commission
November 21, 1990

(3) Is legislation that has a material effect on the Department of Transportation of a general nature as defined by the California Constitution or otherwise not nongeneral legislation as defined by the Ethics Bill?

QUESTION II: Do the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act, including the Act's administrative remedies, apply to Ms. Lee's participating in or attempting to influence decisions regarding Congressional reapportionment for 12 months after she last received payment from the Committee for Congressman Ronald V. Dellums?

The Committee for Congressman Ronald V. Dellums has paid Lee Associates for fundraising work the firm has done for it. The committee is Congressman Dellum's principal campaign committee under federal law. Ms. Lee has received over $250 from the committee through Lee Associates in the last 12 months, and, as stated above, Ms. Lee's interest in the firm is greater than ten percent. See Gov. Code § 87103(c); § 82030.

The following specific questions arise:

A. We understand that the Act applies only if it is "reasonably foreseeable" that the decision will have a "material financial effect" on the source of income to the official. Gov. Code § 87103; In re Owen, supra, at 79-80. Is it "reasonably foreseeable" that Congressional reapportionment will have a "material financial effect" on the Committee for Congressman Ronald V. Dellums?

B. If so, will decisions regarding Congressional reapportionment affect the Dellums Committee in a manner that is indistinguishable from its effect on the public generally?

C. If the Political Reform Act requires Ms. Lee to disqualify herself from any of these decisions, do the remedies of Chapter 3 of the Act apply pursuant to Government Code sections 87102.5 - 87102.6?

(1) Will the effect of Congressional reapportionment on the Dellums Committee be similar to its effect on the public generally?

(2) Will Congressional reapportionment have an impact on "more than a small

number of persons" that is similar to its impact on the Dellums Committee?

(3) Is Congressional reapportionment legislation of a general nature under the California Constitution or otherwise not nongeneral legislation as defined by the Ethics Bill?

We appreciate your prompt attention to these matters.
